author: Origen of Alexandria
interpretation: Origen interprets the 'I' of Romans 7 as Paul speaking in character — adopting the voice of someone still under the law's dominion, not describing his own present Christian experience. The struggle depicted belongs to the unregenerate person or the immature believer who relies on law rather than the Spirit. Origen points to Romans 8 as the resolution: life in the Spirit overcomes the condition described in chapter 7.
context: Origen's reading — that Romans 7 describes the pre-Christian or sub-Christian condition — was the dominant patristic view in both East and West until Augustine changed his mind late in life.

author: Augustine of Hippo (early view)
source_title: Propositions from the Epistle to the Romans
interpretation: In his early career, Augustine agreed with the majority patristic view: Romans 7 describes the person under the law before receiving grace. The struggle between willing and doing reflects the condition of someone who knows the good through the law but lacks the power to perform it. The cry 'wretched man that I am' is resolved by the deliverance announced in verse 25 and the Spirit-filled life of chapter 8.
context: Augustine later reversed this reading during the Pelagian controversy, making Romans 7 one of the rare passages where a single Church Father held two contradictory positions at different stages of his career.

author: Augustine of Hippo (later view)
source_title: Against Two Letters of the Pelagians
interpretation: Against Pelagius, Augustine reversed course: Romans 7 describes the mature Christian, including the apostle Paul himself. Even the regenerate believer, aided by grace, continues to struggle with indwelling sin. The 'I delight in God's law' in verse 22 can only be said by someone in whom the Spirit dwells. Perfection awaits the resurrection; in this life, even saints battle concupiscence and depend moment by moment on grace.
context: Augustine's later reading became enormously influential in Western Christianity. It grounded the Reformation understanding of ongoing sin in the believer (simul iustus et peccator) and shaped Catholic theology's emphasis on the continuing need for sacramental grace throughout life.

author: John Chrysostom
interpretation: Chrysostom insists Paul cannot be speaking of himself as a mature apostle. The passage describes humanity under the law, struggling with the knowledge of sin but lacking the power of the Spirit. Paul uses the first person as a rhetorical device (prosopopoeia) to make the argument more vivid and to avoid seeming to accuse his audience directly. The resolution comes in chapter 8 with the gift of the Spirit.
context: Chrysostom's reading represents the consensus of the Greek Fathers, who generally rejected the idea that a Spirit-filled believer could be described as 'sold under sin' (v. 14). This Eastern view persists in Orthodox theology today.

author: Thomas Aquinas
interpretation: Aquinas follows the later Augustine: Paul speaks as a regenerate person who still experiences the pull of disordered desire (concupiscence). However, Aquinas carefully distinguishes between the involuntary movements of concupiscence, which are not in themselves mortal sins, and the voluntary consent to them, which is. The 'law in my members' refers to the habitual inclination toward sin that remains even after baptism but does not dominate the will aided by grace.
context: Aquinas's nuanced reading allowed Catholic theology to maintain both that baptism genuinely removes the guilt of original sin and that concupiscence remains as a lingering effect — a distinction the Council of Trent would formally define.

author: Martin Luther
interpretation: Luther reads Romans 7 as Paul's autobiography as a believer. The Christian is simultaneously righteous in Christ and a sinner in the flesh. The struggle is not a sign of spiritual failure but of genuine spiritual life — only the regenerate person truly delights in God's law and grieves over sin. Those who feel no such struggle are the ones who should worry. Luther sees this passage as demolishing all self-righteousness: even the apostle Paul could not achieve the good he willed.
context: Luther's reading made Romans 7 a cornerstone of Reformation anthropology. The simul iustus et peccator formula derived from this passage became one of the most distinctive and contested doctrines of Protestant theology.

author: Jacobus Arminius
interpretation: Arminius broke with the Reformed consensus to argue that Romans 7 describes the unregenerate person under conviction of sin, not the regenerate believer. He contended that 'sold under sin' (v. 14) cannot describe someone born of the Spirit, since 1 John insists the one born of God does not practice sin. The passage depicts the highest attainment of moral effort apart from regenerating grace — the person who knows the good but cannot do it without the Spirit.
context: Arminius's reading revived the pre-Augustinian patristic consensus and became standard in Wesleyan-Arminian traditions. It remains a live exegetical option, supported by many contemporary scholars regardless of theological tradition.

author: Rudolf Bultmann
interpretation: Bultmann interprets Romans 7 existentially: the 'I' is not autobiographical but represents the universal human condition as seen retrospectively from faith. The divided self — willing the good but doing evil — reveals the fundamental structure of human existence under the law: the attempt to establish one's own righteousness before God, which inevitably fails. Only the kerygma (the proclaimed gospel) delivers from this existential bondage.
context: Bultmann's demythologizing and existentialist approach read Paul through Heidegger's categories of authentic and inauthentic existence. While his method was controversial, his insight that Paul is making a theological rather than strictly autobiographical point has been widely accepted.

author: James D.G. Dunn
interpretation: Dunn argues Paul describes the believer caught between the 'already' and 'not yet' of salvation. The 'I' is genuinely Paul, but Paul as representative of all Christians who live in the overlap of the ages. The inner delight in God's law (v. 22) marks the regenerate person, while the 'law of sin' (v. 23) reflects the ongoing reality of the old age that has not yet fully passed away. Romans 7 and 8 describe the same person from two perspectives.
context: Dunn's reading mediates between the Augustinian-Reformed and the patristic-Arminian positions by using eschatological categories (already/not yet) rather than anthropological ones (regenerate vs. unregenerate). This approach has gained significant scholarly support.

author: D.A. Carson
interpretation: Carson defends the Augustinian-Reformed reading: Paul describes the ongoing experience of every Christian who takes holiness seriously. The person who feels no tension between the desire for God and the pull of sin is either not yet regenerate or dangerously self-deceived. Carson notes that only someone with the Spirit could say 'I delight in God's law in my inner being' (v. 22), since the unregenerate mind is hostile to God's law (Rom 8:7).
context: Carson's pastoral reading reflects the Reformed tradition's use of Romans 7 to cultivate humility and dependence on grace. By insisting the struggle is normative, this reading guards against perfectionism while maintaining the call to holiness.
